title: Pump Operations Pack 1
description: FREE FOR A LIMITED TIME while testing. Premium Driver/Operator scenarios covering attack lines, wye operations, standpipe and FDC operations, master streams, relay pumping, water shuttle operations, drafting, foam, ladder pipe operations, and combination sprinkler/standpipe support.

hoses: 5-inch LDH supply line from hydrant outlet to Engine 181 intake
200-foot 1¾-inch attack line from Engine 181 pump panel discharge to front door
nozzles: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Grade-level entry, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = number of flowing attack lines × flow per line.
problems.instructorExplanation: The operation has one flowing attack line. The ChiefXD 185 line requires 185 GPM, so the total flow requirement is 185 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add hydrant supply flow separately. The supply line provides water to the engine; the flow requirement is based on the attack line being supplied.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the attack operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 105 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The attack line friction loss rounds to 105 PSI. Adding the 50 PSI nozzle pressure gives a required pump discharge pressure of 155 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: A common mistake is stopping at friction loss only. Pump discharge pressure must include nozzle pressure after friction loss is calculated.

problems.title: Attack Line Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in the attack line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Using the 1¾-inch hose coefficient and the listed flow, the attack line friction loss is 106.1 PSI, rounded to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add nozzle pressure when the question asks only for friction loss. Friction loss is calculated before nozzle pressure is added.

problems.title: Nozzle Pressure
problems.question: What pressure is required at the nozzle?
problems.instructorExplanation: The scenario information panel identifies the nozzle package. The ChiefXD 185 operates at 50 PSI nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not confuse pump discharge pressure with nozzle pressure. Nozzle pressure is only the pressure required at the nozzle.

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop
problems.question: The hydrant static pressure is 70 PSI and the residual pressure is 60 PSI while supplying the current operation. Based on the pressure drop, how many additional equivalent attack lines could be supplied?
formulaBreakdown: Pressure drop = static pressure − residual pressure.
Percent drop = pressure drop ÷ static pressure × 100.
Driver Operator hydrant rule: 10–15% drop supports 2 additional equivalent lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pressure drop is 14.3% of static pressure. Under the standard hydrant pressure-drop rule, that indicates the hydrant can supply two additional equivalent attack lines.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the 10 PSI drop as a 10% drop. The percentage must be based on static pressure: 10 divided by 70 equals 14.3%.

problems.title: Second Identical Attack Line
problems.question: If a second identical attack line is deployed, what would the new total flow requirement be?
formulaBreakdown: New total flow = flow per attack line × number of identical attack lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: A second identical attack line doubles the flow demand. Two 185 GPM lines require a total of 370 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not double the pump pressure to find total flow. Flow is added by line; pressure is calculated separately.

problems.title: Extended Attack Line Pump Pressure
problems.question: If the attack line is extended by one additional 50-foot section, what would the new required pump discharge pressure be?
formulaBreakdown: Extended hose length = 200 ft + 50 ft = 250 ft.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 135 PSI.
PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: Adding 50 feet increases the friction loss. The new rounded friction loss is 135 PSI, and adding 50 PSI nozzle pressure gives a new pump discharge pressure of 185 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add a flat pressure amount for the extension. Recalculate friction loss using the new total hose length, then add nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Apparatus Positioning
problems.question: How far should Engine 181 be positioned from the structure for this type of residential fire attack?
formulaBreakdown: Engine 181 should be positioned at least 30 ft from the structure when practical.
problems.instructorExplanation: For this FirePumpSim residential attack scenario, Engine 181 should be positioned at least 30 ft from the structure when practical. This keeps the apparatus close enough for an efficient attack stretch while maintaining a safer setback from heat, smoke, falling debris, and collapse hazards.
problems.explainMistake: Do not position the engine directly against the structure. Leaving setback space protects the crew and apparatus and keeps the front of the building accessible for hose movement, ladders, and other operations.

hoses: Supply: Hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Main line: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to wye
Branch A: 150 ft of 1.75-inch hose from wye to front door nozzle
Branch B: 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose from wye to Alpha-side nozzle
nozzles: Branch A: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
Branch B: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Grade-level operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Branch A Friction Loss
problems.question: What is the friction loss in Branch A?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Branch A uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Branch A hose length = 150 ft = 1.5 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Branch A is calculated independently from the wye to the nozzle. Using 150 ft of 1.75-inch hose at 185 GPM gives 79.6 PSI, which rounds to 80 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include the main line friction loss or the wye appliance loss when the question asks only for Branch A friction loss. Calculate Branch A by itself from the wye to the nozzle.

problems.title: Branch B Friction Loss
problems.question: What is the friction loss in Branch B?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Branch B uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Branch B hose length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Branch B is the longer branch line. At 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose flowing 185 GPM, the friction loss is 106.1 PSI, which rounds to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the Branch A length for Branch B. Each branch must be calculated separately, and the longer branch will have more friction loss at the same flow.

problems.title: Main Line Friction Loss
problems.question: What is the friction loss in the main line supplying the wye?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Main line uses 2.5-inch hose, so C = 2.
Total main line flow = 185 GPM + 185 GPM = 370 GPM.
Main line hose length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: The main line carries the combined flow of both branches. Since each branch flows 185 GPM, the main line flow is 370 GPM. Using 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose gives 54.8 PSI, rounded to 55 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate the main line using only one branch flow. The main line supplies the wye, so it carries the total flow of both Branch A and Branch B.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to FDC
150 ft of 2.5-inch attack line from standpipe outlet to nozzle
nozzles: 1 1/8-inch smooth bore nozzle, 265 GPM @ 50 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Fire attack operating on the 5th floor

problems.title: Standpipe System Pressure Change
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required for the standpipe system in this operation?
formulaBreakdown: FirePumpSim standard FDC / standpipe system loss applies.
problems.instructorExplanation: The standpipe system adds a standard 25 PSI system loss. This pressure is added to the pump discharge pressure along with friction loss, nozzle pressure, and elevation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not treat the standpipe system as zero loss. The FDC / standpipe system adds pressure demand before the attack line and nozzle are supplied.

problems.title: Elevation Pressure Change
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required due to elevation for the fifth-floor fire attack?
formulaBreakdown: Each floor above the 1st floor adds 10 ft of elevation.
problems.instructorExplanation: The fire attack is operating on the 5th floor. Using the FirePumpSim floor rule, that is 40 ft above grade, which requires 20 PSI of elevation pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the 1st floor as 10 ft of elevation. In this rule, the 1st floor is grade level, so the 5th floor is 40 ft above grade.

problems.title: Total Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the total pump discharge pressure required for the standpipe operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FDC supply hose FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
FDC supply hose FL = 14.0 PSI, rounded to 15 PSI.
Interior attack line FL = 21.1 PSI, rounded to 20 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pump must overcome the FDC supply hose friction loss, the interior attack line friction loss, nozzle pressure, elevation to the fire floor, and the standpipe system loss. Added together, the required pump discharge pressure is 130 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate only the attack line. A standpipe operation also requires the FDC supply hose, elevation, and standpipe system loss to be included in the final pump discharge pressure.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Line A: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
Line B: 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to portable monitor
nozzles: Portable monitor with 1 3/4-inch smooth bore tip
Master stream nozzle pressure: 80 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Level parking lot operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Master Stream Flow
problems.question: Given the portable monitor setup shown, determine the master stream flow.
formulaBreakdown: Smooth bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × d² × √NP.
problems.instructorExplanation: The portable monitor is using a smooth bore tip. Smooth bore flow is calculated using tip diameter and nozzle pressure. A 1 3/4-inch tip at 80 PSI produces approximately 815 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use handline fog nozzle flow values for this question. A portable monitor with a smooth bore tip uses the smooth bore flow formula.

problems.title: Line A Flow
problems.question: How much water is flowing through Line A while supplying the portable monitor?
formulaBreakdown: The monitor is supplied by two equal supply lines.
Line A flow = total monitor flow ÷ number of supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The portable monitor flow is divided between two equal supply lines. Line A carries half of the total monitor flow.
problems.explainMistake: Do not place the full monitor flow in Line A. Because the monitor is supplied by two equal lines, each line carries one-half of the total flow.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the portable monitor operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Flow is split between two equal supply lines.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Line A and Line B use 2.5-inch hose, so C = 2.
Each supply line length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 65 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a portable monitor supplied by two equal lines, calculate the friction loss for one supply line using half of the total monitor flow. Since both lines are equal, either line gives the governing friction loss. Add nozzle pressure and appliance loss to get the required pump discharge pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss using the full monitor flow through one hose. The total flow is split between Line A and Line B before reaching the portable monitor.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
nozzles: Deck gun with 1 3/4-inch smooth bore tip
Master stream nozzle pressure: 80 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Level parking lot operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Deck Gun Flow
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the deck gun operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Smooth bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × d² × √NP.
problems.instructorExplanation: The deck gun is operating with a smooth bore tip. Using the smooth bore flow formula, the master stream flow rounds to 815 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use a handline flow value for a deck gun. A smooth bore master stream is calculated from tip diameter and nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the deck gun operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Deck gun is mounted on the engine, so hose friction loss = 0 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Because the deck gun is mounted directly on Engine 181, there is no attack hose friction loss. The required pump discharge pressure is the master stream nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add attack-line friction loss when the deck gun is mounted on the engine. Only add friction loss when hose is supplying a remote appliance.

problems.title: Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in the deck gun operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Friction loss is calculated for hose lines.
The deck gun is mounted directly on Engine 181.
No attack hose is supplying a remote master stream appliance.
Deck gun hose friction loss = 0 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: A mounted deck gun does not require attack-line friction loss because the stream is supplied directly from the apparatus plumbing.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss for the hydrant supply line as the deck gun friction loss. The question asks for friction loss in the deck gun operation, not the supply line.

problems.title: Nozzle Pressure
problems.question: What pressure is required at the deck gun nozzle?
formulaBreakdown: Deck gun nozzle pressure answer = 80 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The scenario information panel identifies this as a master stream deck gun operation. The required nozzle pressure is 80 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not confuse nozzle pressure with total pump discharge pressure. In this setup they are the same only because there is no hose friction loss, elevation, or appliance loss.

problems.title: Hydrant Pressure Drop
problems.question: The hydrant static pressure is 80 PSI and the residual pressure is 65 PSI while supplying the current operation. Based on the pressure drop, how many additional equivalent deck gun streams could be supplied?
formulaBreakdown: Pressure drop = static pressure − residual pressure.
Percent drop = pressure drop ÷ static pressure × 100.
Driver Operator hydrant rule: 16–25% drop supports 1 additional equivalent stream.
problems.instructorExplanation: The hydrant pressure drop is 18.75 percent. Under the standard pressure-drop rule, that indicates the hydrant can support one additional equivalent stream.
problems.explainMistake: Do not treat the 15 PSI drop as 15 percent. The percentage must be calculated from the static pressure.

problems.title: Additional Attack Line Flow
problems.question: If one additional attack line is placed in service, what is the new total flow requirement for the operation?
formulaBreakdown: New total flow = deck gun flow + attack line flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total fire flow demand is the sum of all flowing devices. Adding one 185 GPM attack line to the 815 GPM deck gun creates a total demand of 1000 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not recalculate the deck gun flow when adding an attack line. Add the new attack-line flow to the existing deck gun flow.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Relay Segment 1: 1000 ft of 5-inch LDH from Engine 181 discharge to Relay Engine intake
Relay Segment 2: 1000 ft of 5-inch LDH from Relay Engine discharge to Attack Engine intake
nozzles: Attack Engine deck gun with 1 3/8-inch smooth bore tip
Master stream nozzle pressure: 80 PSI
appliances: Deck gun mounted on Attack Engine
sceneElements.elevation: Level roadway operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Engine 181 Relay Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required discharge pressure for Engine 181 to supply the relay engine.
formulaBreakdown: Relay PDP = relay hose FL + elevation + desired intake pressure.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Relay Segment 1 uses 5-inch LDH, so C = 0.08.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 must overcome friction loss in the relay line and still deliver the desired intake pressure to the relay engine. With 1000 ft of 5-inch LDH flowing 500 GPM, the friction loss is 20 PSI. Adding the 20 PSI desired intake pressure gives 40 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate only the relay hose friction loss. A relay engine must supply enough pressure to overcome friction loss and still leave the receiving engine with the required intake pressure.

problems.title: Relay Engine Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required discharge pressure for the relay engine to supply the attack engine.
formulaBreakdown: Relay PDP = relay hose FL + elevation + desired intake pressure.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Relay Segment 2 uses 5-inch LDH, so C = 0.08.
problems.instructorExplanation: The relay engine supplies the attack engine through the second relay segment. The second segment has the same hose length and flow as the first segment, so the required discharge pressure is also 40 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the pressure from Engine 181 to this calculation. Each relay engine calculates its own discharge pressure based on the hose it is supplying and the desired intake pressure at the next engine.

problems.title: Attack Engine Pump Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the attack engine supplying the fire attack operation.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
The deck gun is mounted on the attack engine, so attack hose friction loss = 0 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The attack engine is supplying a mounted deck gun. Because there is no attack hose between the pump and the deck gun, the required pump pressure is the master stream nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add relay-line friction loss to the attack engine pump pressure. Relay-line friction loss is handled by the engines supplying the relay segments.

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the relay operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Smooth bore flow formula: GPM = 29.7 × d² × √NP.
problems.instructorExplanation: The relay is supplying one flowing master stream. Using the smooth bore flow formula, the deck gun flow rounds to 500 GPM, which is the total flow requirement for this operation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add flow for each relay segment. The same 500 GPM is moving through the relay system from engine to engine. Total flow is based on the flowing device at the fire.

scenarios.title: Commercial Strip Mall FDC / Standpipe Supply

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
FDC Supply Line A: 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to building FDC
FDC Supply Line B: 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to building FDC
Interior attack line: 150 ft of 2.5-inch hose from standpipe outlet to nozzle
nozzles: 1 1/8-inch smooth bore nozzle, 265 GPM @ 50 PSI
appliances: Building FDC / standpipe system, 25 PSI system loss
sceneElements.elevation: Grade-level commercial occupancy, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: The operation has one flowing interior attack line.
The nozzle is a 1 1/8-inch smooth bore.
FirePumpSim standard flow for a 1 1/8-inch smooth bore is 265 GPM.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total flow requirement is based on the flowing interior attack line supplied through the FDC / standpipe system. The 1 1/8-inch smooth bore line requires 265 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add flow for the two FDC supply hoses. Those hoses are supplying the same standpipe operation, not two separate attack lines.

problems.title: FDC Supply Line Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in each FDC supply line?
formulaBreakdown: The FDC is supplied by two equal supply lines.
Flow per FDC supply line = 265 ÷ 2 = 132.5 GPM.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
FDC supply line length = 100 ft = 1 hundred.
problems.instructorExplanation: Because the two FDC supply lines are equal, the total flow is split evenly between them. Each line carries 132.5 GPM, producing approximately 5 PSI friction loss.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate each FDC supply line using the full 265 GPM. The two equal lines split the flow before entering the FDC.

problems.title: FDC / Standpipe System Loss
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required for the building FDC / standpipe system?
formulaBreakdown: FirePumpSim standard FDC / standpipe system loss applies.
problems.instructorExplanation: The building FDC / standpipe system adds 25 PSI to the pump discharge pressure requirement.
problems.explainMistake: Do not treat the FDC connection as zero loss. The standpipe system loss must be added when calculating pump discharge pressure.

problems.title: Total Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FDC supply line friction loss = 5 PSI.
Interior attack line FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Interior attack line uses 2.5-inch hose, so C = 2.
Interior attack line length = 150 ft = 1.5 hundreds.
Round interior FL to nearest 5 PSI: 20 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pump must overcome the FDC supply hose friction loss, the interior attack line friction loss, nozzle pressure, and the FDC / standpipe system loss. The total required pump discharge pressure is 100 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate only the hose from the engine to the FDC. A complete standpipe supply calculation also includes the interior attack line, nozzle pressure, and standpipe system loss.

problems.title: Sprinkler System Only
problems.question: If the FDC is being used only to supply the building sprinkler system, what pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: For FirePumpSim sprinkler-only FDC support, use the standard sprinkler supply pressure unless a building placard or department SOP gives a different value.
Standard sprinkler-only FDC pump pressure = 150 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: When the FDC is being used only to support the sprinkler system, the FirePumpSim standard setting is 150 PSI. This is a sprinkler-system support pressure rather than an interior attack-line pump pressure calculation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate this as a standpipe handline operation. If the FDC is only supporting sprinklers, use the sprinkler-system FDC supply pressure unless a specific placard or local SOP states otherwise.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Attack Line A: 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose from pump panel discharge to front door firefighter
Attack Line B: 250 ft of 1.75-inch hose from rear discharge to Alpha-side firefighter
nozzles: Attack Line A: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
Attack Line B: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Grade-level residential operation, 0 PSI elevation correction

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = Line A flow + Line B flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: Both attack lines are flowing. Each ChiefXD 185 line requires 185 GPM, so the total flow requirement is 370 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the hydrant supply line as an additional flowing line. The total flow is based on the attack lines being supplied.

problems.title: Attack Line A Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in Attack Line A?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Line A uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Line A hose length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Attack Line A is calculated by itself from the engine discharge to the nozzle. The friction loss rounds to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add nozzle pressure when the question asks only for friction loss. Friction loss is calculated before nozzle pressure is added.

problems.title: Attack Line B Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in Attack Line B?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Line B uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Line B hose length = 250 ft = 2.5 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Attack Line B is longer than Line A, so it has more friction loss at the same flow. The friction loss rounds to 135 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the Line A hose length for Line B. Each line must be calculated separately.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for Engine 181.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
When multiple attack lines are supplied, the highest pressure line governs.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 must be set to supply the highest-pressure flowing attack line. Line B requires 185 PSI, so the pump discharge pressure is 185 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not average the two line pressures. The pump must be set for the line with the highest required pressure.

problems.title: Governing Attack Line
problems.question: Which attack line governs the pump discharge pressure for this operation?
problems.inputLabel: Enter 1 for Line A or 2 for Line B
formulaBreakdown: The highest pressure line governs pump discharge pressure.
problems.instructorExplanation: Line B has the higher required pump pressure because it has greater friction loss. Therefore, Line B governs the pump setting.
problems.explainMistake: Do not choose the closest or shortest line. The governing line is the line requiring the highest pump pressure.

problems.title: Gauge Reading With Lower-Pressure Line Shut Down
problems.question: If the lower-pressure attack line is not flowing while the other attack line remains in operation, what pump discharge pressure will display on the gauge?
formulaBreakdown: Line A is the lower-pressure line.
If Line A is not flowing and Line B remains flowing, Engine 181 must still supply Line B.
Gauge pressure = Line B pump pressure.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pump gauge displays the pressure being supplied by the pump. If the lower-pressure line is shut down but the higher-pressure line remains flowing, the pump still needs to supply 185 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not reduce the pump pressure to the lower line value when that lower-pressure line is shut down. The remaining flowing line still controls the pump setting.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Attack Line A: 250 ft of 1.75-inch hose from Engine 181 pump panel discharge through the front entrance to a third-floor interior attack position
Attack Line B: 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose from Engine 181 rear discharge to a ground-level exterior attack position
nozzles: Attack Line A: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
Attack Line B: ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle, 185 GPM @ 50 PSI
sceneElements.elevation: Attack Line A operates on the 3rd floor. Attack Line B operates from ground level.

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = Line A flow + Line B flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: Both attack lines are flowing. Each ChiefXD 185 line requires 185 GPM, so the total flow requirement is 370 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the hydrant supply line as an additional flowing line. The total flow is based on the attack lines being supplied.

problems.title: Interior Attack Elevation
problems.question: What pump pressure change is required due to elevation for the interior attack line?
problems.instructorExplanation: The interior attack line is operating on the 3rd floor. Using the FirePumpSim floor rule, the 3rd floor is 20 ft above grade, which requires 10 PSI elevation pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not treat the interior line as grade level. The hose enters the building and the attack is operating above ground level.

problems.title: Attack Line A Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in Attack Line A?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Line A uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Line A hose length = 250 ft = 2.5 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Attack Line A is calculated from the engine discharge through the front entrance to the interior attack position. The friction loss rounds to 135 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add elevation or nozzle pressure when the question asks only for friction loss.

problems.title: Attack Line B Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in Attack Line B?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Line B uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
Line B hose length = 200 ft = 2 hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Attack Line B is a ground-level exterior line. At 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose flowing 185 GPM, the friction loss rounds to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the Line A length for Line B. Each attack line must be calculated separately.

problems.title: Governing Attack Line
problems.question: Which attack line governs the pump discharge pressure for this operation?
problems.inputLabel: Enter 1 for Line A or 2 for Line B
formulaBreakdown: Line A PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: Line A governs because it requires the highest pump pressure. It has both greater friction loss and elevation pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not choose the exterior line just because it is visibly flowing upward. The governing line is the line with the highest calculated pump pressure.

problems.title: Required Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for Engine 181.
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 must be set for the highest-pressure flowing line. Line A requires 195 PSI because it includes friction loss, nozzle pressure, and elevation to the third-floor interior attack position.
problems.explainMistake: Do not average the two line pressures. Multiple lines supplied from the same pump are set to the highest required discharge pressure.

hoses: Supply line from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
FDC Supply Line A from Engine 181 discharge to sprinkler FDC inlet A
FDC Supply Line B from Engine 181 discharge to sprinkler FDC inlet B

problems.title: Sprinkler System Support Pressure
problems.question: If the FDC is being used only to support the building sprinkler system, what pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: For FirePumpSim sprinkler-only FDC support, use the standard sprinkler support pressure unless a building placard or department SOP gives a different value.
Standard sprinkler-only FDC pump pressure = 150 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: When Engine 181 is supplying only the building sprinkler system through the FDC, the FirePumpSim standard pump setting is 150 PSI unless a placard or local SOP states otherwise.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate this as an interior handline or standpipe attack operation. Sprinkler-system support uses the sprinkler support pressure.

problems.title: FDC Inlet Supply
problems.question: How many FDC inlets are being supplied during this sprinkler support operation?
formulaBreakdown: The operation shows two separate engine discharges supplying the FDC.
FDC inlet A is supplied by one hose.
FDC inlet B is supplied by one hose.
problems.instructorExplanation: Both FDC inlets are supplied to support the sprinkler system and reduce restriction through a single inlet.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the hydrant supply line as an FDC inlet. The question asks how many FDC inlets are supplied from Engine 181.

problems.title: Third-Floor Sprinkler Support Pressure
problems.question: If the sprinkler system being supplied is known to serve the third floor, what pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: Floor rule: 1st floor = 0 ft, 3rd floor = 20 ft.
Pump pressure = base sprinkler support pressure + elevation.
problems.instructorExplanation: The base FirePumpSim sprinkler support pressure is 150 PSI. Because the sprinkler area is on the third floor, add 10 PSI for elevation, giving a pump discharge pressure of 160 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not leave the pressure at 150 PSI when the supplied sprinkler area is known to be above grade. Add the elevation pressure for the third floor.

problems.title: Total Flow Requirement
problems.question: What is the total flow requirement for the foam operation shown?
formulaBreakdown: The foam eductor controls the operating flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: The foam operation is built around the rated flow of the in-line foam eductor. Since the eductor is rated at 95 GPM, the total flow requirement is 95 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use a standard handline flow unless the nozzle or eductor rating calls for it. In this setup, the foam eductor rating determines the flow.

problems.title: Foam Eductor Inlet Pressure
problems.question: What pressure must be supplied at the foam eductor inlet?
formulaBreakdown: The scenario information gives the foam eductor required inlet pressure.
Foam eductor required inlet pressure = 200 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: The in-line foam eductor must receive its rated inlet pressure to proportion foam correctly. For this operation, that required inlet pressure is 200 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not confuse eductor inlet pressure with total pump discharge pressure. The pump must also overcome friction loss between the engine and the eductor.

problems.title: Pre-Eductor Friction Loss
problems.question: How much friction loss is present in the hose section between Engine 181 and the foam eductor?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Pre-eductor hose uses 1.75-inch hose, so C = 15.5.
problems.instructorExplanation: Only the hose between Engine 181 and the foam eductor is included for this friction loss question. At 95 GPM through 100 ft of 1.75-inch hose, the friction loss rounds to 15 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include the hose after the eductor when calculating pre-eductor friction loss. The question asks only for the hose section before the foam eductor.

problems.title: Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Determine the required pump discharge pressure for the foam operation shown.
formulaBreakdown: Foam operation PP = pre-eductor FL + required eductor inlet pressure + elevation.
Required foam eductor inlet pressure = 200 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 must overcome friction loss before the foam eductor and still deliver the required inlet pressure to the eductor. The required pump discharge pressure is 215 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not set the pump at only 200 PSI. The eductor needs 200 PSI at its inlet, so the pump must also overcome the friction loss between the engine and the eductor.

problems.title: Foam Concentrate Needed
problems.question: How many gallons of foam concentrate are needed for the assigned operating period?
problems.instructorExplanation: Foam concentrate use is based on total foam solution flow and the selected foam percentage. At 95 GPM for 10 minutes using 3% foam, the operation requires 28.5 gallons of concentrate.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the water supply hose size or pump pressure to calculate concentrate use. Foam concentrate is calculated from flow, time, and foam percentage.

problems.title: Attack Line Pump Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying one 200 ft 1.75-inch attack line with a ChiefXD 185 fog nozzle operating at 185 GPM @ 50 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: For the attack line, Engine 181 must overcome the friction loss in the 200 ft 1.75-inch hose and provide the required nozzle pressure. The correct pump discharge pressure is 155 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include the hard suction or dump tank in the attack line pump pressure calculation. The pump pressure question is based on the discharge hose, nozzle pressure, and any elevation or appliance loss.

problems.title: Usable Tender Water
problems.question: Each water tender carries 3000 gallons. Using the 90% usable water rule, how many gallons are usable from one tender?
problems.instructorExplanation: For shuttle calculations, only 90% of the tender capacity is counted as usable water. A 3000-gallon tender provides 2700 usable gallons.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the full tank capacity when calculating shuttle flow. The FirePumpSim tender shuttle rule uses 90% usable water.

problems.title: Single Tender Shuttle Flow
problems.question: One tender provides 2700 usable gallons and has a 12-minute shuttle cycle time. What sustained flow can one tender provide?
formulaBreakdown: Tender shuttle GPM = usable gallons / cycle time.
problems.instructorExplanation: A tender shuttle flow rate is based on usable water divided by total cycle time. One tender can sustain 225 GPM in this setup.
problems.explainMistake: Do not divide the full 3000-gallon capacity by the cycle time. Use the 90% usable water value.

problems.title: Number of Tenders Needed
problems.question: The operation needs a sustained water supply of 450 GPM. If each tender can sustain 225 GPM, how many tenders are needed?
formulaBreakdown: Tenders needed = required sustained flow / flow per tender.
problems.instructorExplanation: Two tenders are needed because each tender can sustain 225 GPM and the operation requires 450 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count only the tender currently dumping. Shuttle operations require enough tenders cycling to maintain the required sustained flow.

problems.title: Dump Tank Water Duration
problems.question: If the portable dump tank contains 2500 gallons and Engine 181 is flowing only the 185 GPM attack line, how long will the dump tank supply last with no refill?
problems.instructorExplanation: With no refill, the dump tank duration is the available water divided by the attack line flow. At 185 GPM, 2500 gallons lasts about 13.5 minutes.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use tender cycle time for this question. The question asks how long the water already in the dump tank will last if no additional water is added.

problems.title: Engine 181 Relay Pump Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is pumping through 1500 ft of 5-inch LDH to Relay Engine 2. The relay flow is 265 GPM and the desired intake pressure at Relay Engine 2 is 20 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should Engine 181 set?
formulaBreakdown: Relay PDP = relay hose FL + elevation + desired intake pressure.
FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 10 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 must overcome the friction loss in the long LDH relay segment and still deliver 20 PSI intake pressure to Relay Engine 2. The correct pump discharge pressure is 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add nozzle pressure to a relay engine calculation. Engine 181 is only moving water to the next engine, so use relay hose friction loss plus the desired intake pressure.

problems.title: Relay Engine 2 Pump Pressure
problems.question: Relay Engine 2 is pumping through 1500 ft of 5-inch LDH to Relay Engine 3. The relay flow is 265 GPM and the desired intake pressure at Relay Engine 3 is 20 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should Relay Engine 2 set?
formulaBreakdown: Relay PDP = relay hose FL + elevation + desired intake pressure.
FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 10 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Relay Engine 2 is not supplying the nozzle directly. It is supplying Relay Engine 3, so the required pump discharge pressure is the relay segment friction loss plus the desired intake pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use attack-line pump pressure for Relay Engine 2. A relay engine only needs to move water to the next intake while maintaining the target intake pressure.

problems.title: Relay Engine 3 Pump Pressure
problems.question: Relay Engine 3 is pumping through 1500 ft of 5-inch LDH to Attack Engine 4. The relay flow is 265 GPM and the desired intake pressure at Attack Engine 4 is 20 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should Relay Engine 3 set?
formulaBreakdown: Relay PDP = relay hose FL + elevation + desired intake pressure.
FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 10 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Relay Engine 3 must overcome the friction loss in the final relay segment and deliver the target intake pressure to Attack Engine 4. The correct pump discharge pressure is 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add nozzle pressure at Relay Engine 3. The attack engine handles the attack-line pump pressure separately.

problems.title: Attack Engine Pump Pressure
problems.question: Attack Engine 4 is supplying a 250 ft 2.5-inch attack line with a 1 1/8-inch smooth bore nozzle flowing 265 GPM @ 50 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should Attack Engine 4 set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 35 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: Attack Engine 4 receives water from the relay system and then supplies the attack line. Its pump discharge pressure is based on the attack-line friction loss plus nozzle pressure.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the relay engine pressure for the attack engine. The attack engine must calculate the pressure needed for the attack hose and nozzle.

problems.title: Total Relay Hose Distance
problems.question: There are three relay hose segments between Engine 181 and Attack Engine 4. Each relay segment is 1500 ft. What is the total relay hose distance between Engine 181 and Attack Engine 4?
formulaBreakdown: Total relay hose distance = number of relay segments x length per segment.
problems.instructorExplanation: The relay operation uses three long LDH segments between Engine 181 and Attack Engine 4. The total relay hose distance is 4500 ft.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include the hydrant-to-Engine 181 supply hose or the final attack line. This question asks only for the relay hose distance between Engine 181 and Attack Engine 4.

problems.title: Combined Dump Tank Water Duration
problems.question: The two portable dump tanks are connected by a jet siphon and function as one water source. If each dump tank contains 2500 gallons and Engine 181 is flowing 185 GPM, how long will the combined dump tank supply last with no refill?

problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate using only one dump tank when the jet siphon connects both tanks into a single water source.

problems.title: Drafting Tank Identification
problems.question: Engine 181 is drafting through the large black hard suction from only one dump tank. How many dump tanks are directly connected to Engine 181 by hard suction?

Direct drafting tanks connected to Engine 181 = 1.

problems.explainMistake: Do not count both dump tanks as directly drafted. Only the tank connected to Engine 181 by the large hard suction is directly drafted.

problems.title: Attack Line Pump Pressure

formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: The pump pressure for the attack line is based on attack-line friction loss plus nozzle pressure. The hard suction, pond, and floating strainer are part of the intake side and are not added to the discharge pressure calculation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add hard suction or drafting setup components to the attack-line pump pressure. Use the attack hose friction loss plus nozzle pressure.

problems.title: Attack Line Friction Loss
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying 185 GPM through 200 ft of 1.75-inch attack hose. What is the attack line friction loss?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
Round FL to nearest 5 PSI: 105 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: At 185 GPM through 200 ft of 1.75-inch hose, the friction loss rounds to 105 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not include nozzle pressure when the question asks only for friction loss.

problems.title: Total Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 is operating one ChiefXD 185 nozzle from the drafting setup. What is the total flow from Engine 181?
problems.instructorExplanation: Only one attack line is flowing, so the total flow is the rated flow of that nozzle: 185 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the hard suction as a separate discharge flow. It is the intake supply path from the pond to the pump.

problems.title: Static Lift Pressure
problems.question: If the water surface at the pond is 8 ft below the pump intake, what lift pressure must the drafting setup overcome?
formulaBreakdown: Lift pressure = lift height × 0.5 PSI per foot.
problems.instructorExplanation: The drafting setup must overcome the vertical lift from the water surface to the pump intake. At 8 ft of lift, the pressure equivalent is 4 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate lift from the length of the hard suction hose. Lift is based on vertical distance from the water surface to the pump intake.

problems.title: Static Water Duration
problems.question: If the accessible static water supply contains 12000 usable gallons and Engine 181 is flowing 185 GPM, how long will the water supply last?
problems.instructorExplanation: Water supply duration is the usable water volume divided by the operating flow. At 185 GPM, 12000 gallons lasts about 65 minutes.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use pump pressure to calculate water duration. Duration is based only on available gallons and flow.

problems.title: Additional Attack Line Flow
problems.question: If a second identical ChiefXD 185 attack line is added, what would the total flow from Engine 181 be?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = flow per line × number of lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: Two identical ChiefXD 185 attack lines would flow a total of 370 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not change the nozzle flow because the engine is drafting. The total flow is based on the number of operating nozzles.

problems.title: Drafting Intake Setup
problems.question: How many hard suction hoses are shown drafting from the pond into Engine 181?

Hard suction hoses drafting from pond = 1.
problems.instructorExplanation: Engine 181 is drafting through one large hard suction hose with a floating strainer at the water source.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the attack hose as a drafting hose. The attack hose is connected to a discharge, while the hard suction is connected to the intake.

problems.title: Total Monitor Flow
problems.question: The portable monitor is flowing 500 GPM. What is the total flow for the monitor operation?
formulaBreakdown: The monitor operation is flowing 500 GPM.
problems.instructorExplanation: The total flow is the flow being delivered through the portable monitor. In this operation, the monitor flow is 500 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the two supply line flows together unless each line flow is given separately. The monitor total flow is already stated as 500 GPM.

problems.title: Flow Per Supply Line
problems.question: The portable monitor is supplied by two equal supply lines and the total monitor flow is 500 GPM. How much flow is carried by each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: Flow per line = total monitor flow / number of supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: A portable monitor supplied by two equal lines splits the total flow between the lines. Each line carries 250 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss using the full 500 GPM in each line. Each supply line carries half of the total monitor flow.

problems.title: Friction Loss Per Supply Line
problems.question: Each monitor supply line is 300 ft of 2.5-inch hose flowing 250 GPM. What is the friction loss in each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C × (GPM / 100)^2 × hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each supply line carries 250 GPM. At 250 GPM through 300 ft of 2.5-inch hose, friction loss rounds to 40 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the total 500 GPM to calculate friction loss for one line. The total flow is split between the two supply lines.

problems.title: Elevation Pressure
problems.question: The portable monitor is positioned 60 ft above Engine 181. What elevation pressure must be added?
formulaBreakdown: Elevation pressure = elevation height × 0.5 PSI per foot.
problems.instructorExplanation: Because the monitor is uphill from Engine 181, elevation pressure must be added. A 60 ft elevation gain requires 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not subtract elevation pressure for an uphill operation. Uphill hose lays require additional pressure.

problems.title: Portable Monitor Pump Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying an uphill portable monitor with two 300 ft 2.5-inch supply lines. The monitor flows 500 GPM total, each line carries 250 GPM, nozzle pressure is 80 PSI, elevation pressure is 30 PSI, and monitor appliance loss is 25 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should be set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss per supply line = 40 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For the portable monitor operation, calculate each supply line separately. Since both lines are equal, either line gives the same friction loss. Add friction loss, monitor nozzle pressure, elevation, and appliance loss for a final pump pressure of 175 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add friction loss for both supply lines together. The two equal supply lines split the flow, and the pump pressure is based on the pressure required by one supply path.

problems.title: Hydrant Supply Line Count
problems.question: How many hydrant supply lines are connected from the hydrant to Engine 181?
formulaBreakdown: The scene shows one hydrant supply hose connected to Engine 181.
problems.instructorExplanation: Only one hydrant supply line is connected from the hydrant to Engine 181. The two blue hoses are monitor supply lines from Engine 181 to the portable monitor.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the two blue monitor supply hoses as hydrant supply lines. They are discharge lines going uphill to the monitor.

problems.question: Each ladder supply line is 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose flowing 375 GPM. What is the friction loss in each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each supply line carries 375 GPM. At 375 GPM through 200 ft of 2.5-inch hose, friction loss rounds to 55 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss from both equal supply lines together. Calculate one supply path because the lines are parallel.

problems.title: Ladder Pipe Elevation Pressure
problems.question: The ladder pipe nozzle is 60 ft above Engine 181. What elevation pressure must be added?
formulaBreakdown: Elevation pressure = elevation height x 0.5 PSI per foot.
problems.instructorExplanation: The ladder pipe is above Engine 181, so elevation pressure must be added. A 60 ft elevation gain requires 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not subtract elevation pressure for an elevated ladder pipe. Pressure must be added for uphill elevation.

problems.title: Ladder Pipe Pump Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the ladder truck with two 200 ft 2.5-inch supply lines. The ladder pipe flows 750 GPM total, each supply line carries 375 GPM, friction loss is 55 PSI, nozzle pressure is 80 PSI, elevation is 30 PSI, and ladder waterway appliance loss is 25 PSI. What pump discharge pressure should Engine 181 set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss per supply line = 55 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a ladder pipe supplied through two equal lines, calculate one supply path. Add supply line friction loss, ladder pipe nozzle pressure, elevation pressure, and ladder waterway appliance loss. Engine 181 should pump 190 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss of both blue supply lines together. Parallel supply lines split the flow, so one supply path governs the pump pressure.

problems.title: Ladder Truck Supply Hose Count
problems.question: How many supply hoses from Engine 181 feed the ladder truck?
formulaBreakdown: The scene shows two supply hoses from Engine 181 to the ladder truck.
problems.instructorExplanation: The hydrant supplies Engine 181, and Engine 181 supplies the ladder truck with two separate supply hoses.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the yellow hydrant supply hose as a ladder truck supply hose. The ladder truck is fed by the two blue hoses from Engine 181.

problems.title: Total Standpipe Flow
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying two interior standpipe attack lines. Each line flows 265 GPM. What is the total flow for this standpipe operation?
formulaBreakdown: Total flow = attack line A flow + attack line B flow.
problems.instructorExplanation: The engine is supplying two interior standpipe attack lines. Add both attack line flows to get the total system flow of 530 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count only one attack line. Both interior lines are supplied through the standpipe system.

problems.title: Flow Per FDC Supply Line
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying the hotel FDC with two equal supply lines. The total standpipe flow is 530 GPM. How much flow is carried by each FDC supply line?
formulaBreakdown: Flow per FDC line = total standpipe flow / number of FDC supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The two FDC supply lines split the total standpipe flow equally. Each FDC supply line carries 265 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate each FDC supply line using the full 530 GPM. The two supply lines share the flow.

problems.title: FDC Supply Line Friction Loss
problems.question: Each FDC supply line is 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose flowing 265 GPM. What is the friction loss in each FDC supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each FDC supply line carries 265 GPM. At 265 GPM through 100 ft of 2.5-inch hose, friction loss rounds to 15 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not use the total 530 GPM in one FDC supply line. Each line carries half of the total flow.

problems.title: Standpipe Elevation Pressure
problems.question: The fire room is on the 8th floor. Using 5 PSI per floor above the 1st floor, what elevation pressure must be added?
formulaBreakdown: Elevation pressure = floors above 1st floor x 5 PSI.
8th floor is 7 floors above the 1st floor.
problems.instructorExplanation: The 8th floor is seven floors above the 1st floor. At 5 PSI per floor, the standpipe operation requires 35 PSI for elevation.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add 5 PSI for the 1st floor. The 1st floor is the baseline elevation.

problems.title: Standpipe Pump Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 is supplying the hotel FDC with two 100 ft 2.5-inch lines. The fire is on the 8th floor. Each interior attack line is 150 ft of 2.5-inch hose flowing 265 GPM through a 1 1/8-inch smooth bore nozzle. Use 15 PSI FDC supply line friction loss, 20 PSI interior attack line friction loss, 50 PSI nozzle pressure, 35 PSI elevation, and 25 PSI standpipe/FDC loss. What pump discharge pressure should Engine 181 set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss = FDC supply line FL + interior attack line FL.
problems.instructorExplanation: For this standpipe operation, calculate one complete supply path from Engine 181 to the FDC, through the standpipe, and through the interior attack line. Add the FDC supply line friction loss, interior hose friction loss, nozzle pressure, elevation, and standpipe/FDC loss. The required pump pressure is 145 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add friction loss for both equal FDC supply lines together. Parallel supply lines split the flow, and one complete supply path governs the pump pressure.

formulaBreakdown: The scene shows two hoses from Engine 181 to the hotel FDC.
problems.instructorExplanation: The hydrant supplies Engine 181, and Engine 181 supplies the hotel FDC with two separate hose lines.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the hydrant supply hose as an FDC supply hose. The FDC is supplied by the two hoses from Engine 181.
